reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Strong source-grounded mechanics for the GitHub App lifecycle (PR comment creation/update, temporary config creation with isTemporaryForPr, auto-delete via deleteStackAfterPrClose) — all traceable to the provided console-app source.

Excellent use of tables throughout: the three-shape summary table, naming convention table, cleanup strategy table, and combining-patterns table give scannable structure that the target audience can quickly act on.
Comprehensive, opinionated FAQ (10 questions) covering Stacktape-specific, AWS-cost, and comparison questions — good category balance per AEO rules.
Concrete, single-command bash blocks per scenario with no comment-polluted alternatives — correct shell block discipline throughout.
Decision-support is present for opt-in features (auto-delete, serverless-only previews, feature branches vs PR previews) with tradeoffs and opinionated recommendations.
"When to use" and "When NOT to use" are peer H2 sections as required.
No forbidden patterns: no fenced YAML Stacktape config, no StacktapeConfig typed style, no fenced TypeScript with defineConfig, no hand-authored YAML tabs.
problems: Stage name maximum length is stated as '12 characters' in the naming table. The project CLAUDE.md memory records max 10 chars, and neither provided source file (github-app/utils.ts, GitOps.tsx) contains an explicit validation of the upper bound. This is a potentially inaccurate numeric claim not grounded in the provided source.

The section-level instruction explicitly says 'When discussing GitHub or GitLab integrations, ground the mechanics in console-app/server/lambdas/github-app source'. GitLab is never mentioned on the page. The page reads as though GitHub is the only git provider.

The section-level instruction says 'Distinguish CodeBuild-based runners from EC2 runners explicitly.' The page has no mention of build runner types when describing custom CI/CD paths — a stated requirement for this section is unmet.

The FAQ answer for 'Can I use different configuration for preview stages vs production?' refers to directives and conditional TypeScript config but provides no code example — the concept is more complex than the two-sentence answer implies, and the page never shows what stage-conditional config looks like in practice.

The 'How PR previews work with custom CI/CD' section uses PR_NUMBER as the environment variable name without stating which CI systems expose it (GitHub Actions exposes it as github.event.pull_request.number, not PR_NUMBER) — the variable name is not a standard cross-provider variable and may mislead beginners.
mandatoryFixes: Verify the stage name maximum length against a ground-truth source (schema validator, CLI source, or Console form validation) and correct the table value. The memory records 10 characters; the page claims 12. If neither can be confirmed from source, remove the specific number and write 'keep stage names short' with a qualitative warning only.

Add a short paragraph or callout noting whether GitLab is supported under the GitOps path. If GitLab GitOps is not supported, say so explicitly ('GitOps auto-deployment currently supports GitHub only'). If it is supported, add a parallel 'How PR previews work with GitLab' sub-section grounded in source. The section instructions require this distinction.

Add a sentence in the custom CI/CD subsections (at minimum a callout or cross-link) distinguishing CodeBuild-based runners from EC2/self-hosted runners, as required by the section-level instructions. At minimum: 'When using Stacktape build runners in your custom pipeline, you can choose between CodeBuild-managed runners and EC2 self-hosted runners — see [Build Runners](/ci-cd-and-gitops/build-runners) for details.'

Replace PR_NUMBER with the actual environment variable name(s) for the relevant CI systems, or replace with a generic placeholder (e.g., YOUR_PR_NUMBER) and add a prose note explaining where to find it per system (e.g., GitHub Actions: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}, GitLab CI: $CI_MERGE_REQUEST_IID). An incorrect env var name in a copy-paste example will immediately break a beginner's pipeline.
optionalImprovements: Add a minimal TypeScript config snippet (inside a <CodeBlock intellisense> block) showing how to branch on stage name for preview-vs-production sizing — the FAQ promises this pattern exists but the page never demonstrates it, leaving the reader to figure it out alone.

The 'Setting up with GitOps' subsection under long-lived branches is very brief (two sentences, one link). Add the concrete steps from the Console UI (trigger type selection, branch selection, stage name field) the same way the PR preview subsection does — the asymmetry makes the PR preview section feel more authoritative.

Consider adding a 'Gotchas' or 'Common mistakes' callout box summarizing the two or three most common beginner errors (branch name with slash used as stage name, forgetting auto-delete, stale stages from abandoned features). These are high-value for the target reader and currently scattered across warnings and FAQ.

The page never links to `stacktape dev` (dev mode) as an alternative for single developers, even though 'When NOT to use' mentions that solo devs may not need per-branch stages. A one-sentence pointer to dev mode would complete that recommendation.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: PR lifecycle mechanics are tightly grounded in source: the three-step flow (PR opened → temp config created for push tracking → PR closed → temp config deleted + stack deleted) maps directly to `createGitConfigurationForPR`, `isTemporaryForPr`, and `deleteGitConfigurationForPR` in utils.ts.
Stage name derivation (`pr-${pullRequestNumber}`) is accurately sourced from the utils.ts line: `stage: \`pr-${pullRequestNumber}\``.
The GitOps.tsx source confirms exactly two trigger types ('PR Opened' and 'Push to Branch'), and the page represents both accurately without inventing additional variants.
Shell command blocks are clean: one command per block, no explanatory `#` comments inside the block, no copy-button traps.
Decision-support content is strong throughout — when-to-use, when-not-to-use, and cost sections each give the reader enough to act, not just configure.
Tables are used well for comparisons (shapes, naming patterns, cleanup strategies) — these will extract well as cited snippets.
FAQ covers all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS-general, and comparison/choosing), and all 10 answers are substantive, not padding.
No forbidden style violations: no YAML Stacktape config blocks, no `StacktapeConfig` type imports, no `defineConfig` in fenced TypeScript blocks, no marketing language.
problems: Stage name maximum length is stated as 12 characters, but this number is not present in the provided source files (`utils.ts` and `GitOps.tsx` both omit any length validation). The CLAUDE.md developer context for the same codebase says 10 characters. Shipping the wrong limit here causes real deployment failures.

The PR comment lifecycle description claims 'success with quick links to deployed resources' as a PR comment state, but the provided source only shows `getPendingDeploymentMarkdownMessage` and `getFailedDeploymentMarkdownMessage`. A success-state comment update is not visible in the source excerpt, making this a partially ungrounded claim.

The exact option label 'Delete stack after pull request is merged or closed' is described as shown in the Console, but `CreateGitOpsConfigModal` — the component that would render this label — is not among the provided source files. The database field is `deleteStackAfterPrClose`; the UI label cannot be verified from the available source.

The `--configPath` flag is absent from every shell example on the page. For the majority of real projects where the config file is not at the default path, these commands will silently use the wrong config or fail. A brief mention of `--configPath` or a note that it is omitted for brevity would prevent confusion at the copypaste moment.
optionalImprovements: Verify and correct the stage name maximum length. CLAUDE.md developer context says 10 characters; the page says 12. Cross-check against the runtime validator in `src/domain/**` or the Console's `CreateGitOpsConfigModal` form validation, and update the table to match the authoritative value.

Hedge or narrow the 'success with quick links' claim: change 'showing pending status, success with quick links to deployed resources, or failure details' to 'showing pending status or failure details' (and optionally success if grounded from a success message helper not in the provided excerpt) to avoid asserting behavior not visible in the source.

Add a one-line note in each shell example section acknowledging `--configPath` (e.g., 'Add `--configPath path/to/stacktape.ts` if your config file is not in the default location') so copiers with non-default project layouts don't silently deploy the wrong stack.

The 'Combining patterns' section is slightly redundant with the overview table at the top of 'How the pattern works'. Consider either merging them or making the 'Combining patterns' section additive by including a concrete Console setup sequence (e.g., 'Create three GitOps configurations: one Push-to-Branch for main→prod, one Push-to-Branch for develop→staging, one PR-Opened for PRs targeting main').

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Clear answer-first opening paragraph that explains the pattern and why teams use it.
Good scannability through short H2s, comparison tables, and separate sections for long-lived branches, PR previews, feature branches, cost, and cleanup.
Shell examples follow the copy-button rule: one command per block with no explanatory comments inside.
FAQ is broad and useful, mixing Stacktape-specific, AWS-cost, debugging, migration, and GitOps-vs-CI questions.
problems: Several Stacktape-specific claims are not grounded in the provided source files, especially stage naming constraints, maximum stage length, no Stacktape-imposed stage limit, multi-region PR preview setup, shared database guidance, deployment hooks, and stage-specific directives.

The page overstates GitOps Console flow details beyond the provided Console source. The source shows a GitOps table, a New GitOps configuration button, trigger labels, stage labels, and deletion modal, but not the modal fields or exact setup sequence described.

The PR preview lifecycle is mostly aligned with github-app source, but the auto-delete behavior is stated more strongly than the provided source supports. The shown source stores deleteStackAfterPrClose on temporary PR configs, while the provided deletion path itself does not show the conditional check.

The section 'The stage name is the only input that separates one deployed stack from another within the same project and region' is too broad. The source queries stack operations by AWS account connection, AWS account ID, region, project name, and stage.
Stage naming section gives exact constraints and a Console-specific 12-character maximum without any supporting source in the files provided.

Cost and cleanup guidance is useful but partially generic; claims about serverless previews costing nearly nothing, RDS/ECS hourly costs, and budget alerts are not grounded in the provided source files, though some may be acceptable as general AWS background in FAQ.
Some FAQ answers introduce unsupported Stacktape-specific features or mechanics, such as defining shared databases in a separate stack and referencing them with $ResourceParam from consuming stages.
The page does not explicitly distinguish CodeBuild-based runners from EC2 runners, despite the human-maintained section instruction requiring that distinction.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or source-ground the exact stage naming constraints, including lowercase-only, minimum 2 characters, and 12-character Console maximum. If those constraints are not in the provided source, replace them with cautious wording or omit the table.

Revise the stack isolation explanation to include the dimensions shown in source-backed operations: AWS account/account connection, AWS region, project name, and stage, rather than saying stage is the only separating input.

Narrow Console workflow claims to what the provided source supports, or add source files for CreateGitOpsConfigModal/server routes before describing modal fields such as 'Push to Branch', 'PR Opened', and 'Delete stack after pull request is merged or closed'.
Address the required CodeBuild-based runner vs EC2 runner distinction, or add a clearly scoped note that the provided source for this page does not expose runner selection details.

Fix unsupported FAQ claims about multi-region PR preview configurations, shared databases between stages, deployment hooks, stage-specific directives, and no Stacktape-imposed stage limit unless matching source files are added.

State the corrected PR preview facts explicitly: GitHub PR-open handling derives stage as `pr-{pullRequestNumber}`, creates a temporary push-to-branch configuration for the PR branch, stores `pullRequestNumber`, `pullRequestTargetBranch`, and `isTemporaryForPr`, and PR-close handling finds that temporary configuration for deletion.
optionalImprovements: Move 'When to use this pattern' and 'When NOT to use this pattern' closer to the top, before setup details, to help readers decide before configuring.
Add a compact table comparing GitOps Console and custom CI/CD: lifecycle automation, PR comments, required pipeline code, and when each is better.
Make the first paragraph of 'Combining patterns' self-contained by naming 'the stacks-per-branch pattern' instead of opening with 'Most teams combine two or three patterns.'
Trim or qualify exact deployment-time claims such as '10-15 minutes' unless a source file supports that range.
Where CLI commands use `npx stacktape`, link the first deploy/delete command references to the relevant CLI pages if those routes exist.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is generally aligned with the provided GitHub App source for PR-opened mapping to `pr-{number}` and push-to-branch trigger names, but it overstates several Console and PR lifecycle mechanics that are not grounded in the supplied source. The highest-risk problems are around what uniquely identifies a stack, whether PR branch pushes redeploy previews, and exact stage naming constraints.

issues.statement: “The stage name is the only input that separates one deployed stack from another within the same project and region.”
issues.reason: The provided source keys stack operations by connected AWS account/AWS account ID, region, project name, and stage. Within the same project and region, the AWS account/account connection is also part of the identity in the source, so saying the stage name is the only input is too broad.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the claim to: “Within the same project, AWS account, and AWS region, the stage name separates one deployed stack from another.”

evidence.quote:
where: {
      accountConnectionId: connectedAwsAccount.id,
      awsAccountId: connectedAwsAccount.awsAccountId!,
      region: targetRegion,
      projectName: project.name,
      stage
    }

issues.statement: “Subsequent pushes — A temporary internal configuration is created so that pushes to the PR branch also trigger redeployments to the same `pr-{number}` stage.”
issues.reason: The source does show temporary PR configurations being created with `deployOnGitEvent: 'PUSHED_TO_BRANCH'`, but the push-event lookup explicitly excludes temporary PR configurations. Based on the supplied source, the stated redeploy-on-push behavior is not supported and appears contradicted by the push-event query.
issues.suggestedFix: Rewrite cautiously: “The source creates a temporary PR GitOps configuration for the PR branch, but the provided handler code does not show subsequent push events selecting those temporary configurations. Avoid promising automatic redeploys on PR branch pushes unless additional source confirms that path.”

issues.statement: “Stage names must follow these constraints: lowercase letters, digits, and hyphens; minimum length 2 characters; maximum length 12 characters (enforced in Console GitOps configurations).”
issues.reason: The supplied source shows stage values being displayed and generated for PR previews, but it does not include validation logic for these exact allowed characters or length limits. The exact constraints and the “enforced in Console GitOps configurations” claim need a validation source.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the exact constraint table unless the relevant stage validation source is added. Keep only sourced examples such as `prod`, `staging`, and generated PR stages like `pr-{number}`.

issues.suggestedFix: Either add the `CreateGitOpsConfigModal` source to ground the UI label, or phrase it without the quoted UI text: “PR GitOps configurations carry a `deleteStackAfterPrClose` setting in the GitHub App source.”

issues.suggestedFix: Limit the claim to what the source shows: the handler creates pending PR comments for preview deployments/deletions and updates the comment on orchestration failure. Add success-update source before documenting success quick links.

issues.statement: “PR closed or merged — If auto-delete is enabled, the handler starts a stack deletion for the preview stage and removes the temporary configuration.”
issues.reason: The source shows deletion configurations being found for `PULL_REQUEST_CLOSED` by owner, repository, branch, organization, deploy event, and PR number. The provided lookup does not include `deleteStackAfterPrClose`, so the conditional “if auto-delete is enabled” gate is not visible in the supplied source.
issues.suggestedFix: Write more narrowly: “On PR close, the provided GitHub App source can find the temporary PR configuration and start a delete operation; the provided source also stores a `deleteStackAfterPrClose` flag, but the gating logic is not shown here.”

      operationType: 'delete',

issues.statement: “Unlike PR previews, feature stages don’t auto-create from a trigger — you deploy them explicitly when you want a live environment for a long-running feature.”
issues.reason: The provided GitHub App source supports configured push-to-branch deployments for any matched branch name. A feature branch can be deployed automatically if a GitOps configuration exists for that branch, so the statement is too absolute.
issues.suggestedFix: Clarify the distinction: “The provided GitOps source supports configured push-to-branch deployments. It does not show a wildcard feature-branch trigger that automatically creates stages for every new branch, so teams commonly deploy feature stages explicitly or configure a specific push-to-branch rule.”

issues.statement: The custom CI/CD examples use `--autoConfirmOperation` and state that it skips the interactive confirmation prompt.
issues.reason: The page source list does not include the CLI command implementation or CLI command definitions, so this flag behavior cannot be verified from the supplied sources. This may be correct, but it is not grounded in the provided files.
issues.suggestedFix: Verify against the CLI source for `deploy` and `delete`, or remove the flag-specific explanation and link readers to `/cli/deploy` and `/cli/delete` for CI-safe options.

positiveFindings: The draft correctly uses the documented route `/ci-cd-and-gitops/stacks-per-git-branch-pattern` and all checked cross-links point to routes present in the navigation index.
The PR preview stage format `pr-{number}` is grounded in `utils.ts`, which sets `stage: `pr-${pullRequestNumber}`` for pull request opened events.
The draft’s distinction between `PR Opened` and `Push to Branch` trigger types is grounded in both the GitHub App handler and the Console GitOps table badges.
The draft correctly links `stacktape debug:logs` to `/cli/debug-logs` instead of inventing a `stacktape logs` command.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is useful and mostly uses valid site links, but it overstates several GitOps and stage-behavior details beyond the two provided source files. The highest-risk problems are PR-preview redeploy mechanics, stage naming constraints, Console option labels, and broad claims about Stacktape stage isolation/limits that are not grounded in the supplied sources.

issues.statement: “Subsequent pushes — A temporary internal configuration is created so that pushes to the PR branch also trigger redeployments to the same `pr-{number}` stage.”
issues.reason: The source does show creation of a temporary PR Git deployment configuration, but the provided webhook action selection explicitly excludes temporary PR configurations for `PUSHED_TO_BRANCH` events. The draft’s claim that subsequent pushes trigger redeployments through that temporary configuration contradicts the visible filtering logic in the provided source.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the wording to what the source directly shows: on PR open, Stacktape creates a temporary Git deployment configuration for the PR branch and `pr-{number}` stage. Do not claim that subsequent pushes redeploy the preview unless another source file proves that path.

issues.statement: “Stage names must follow these constraints: lowercase letters, digits, and hyphens; minimum length 2 characters; maximum length 12 characters (enforced in Console GitOps configurations).”
issues.reason: The provided Console GitOps and GitHub App source files do not show any stage-name validation or max-length enforcement. This is a concrete Stacktape constraint that would change what readers type, so it needs a source from config validation or Console form validation.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the exact constraints from this page or replace with cautious guidance to keep derived stage names short and sanitized. Add exact constraints only if the relevant validation source is provided.

issues.statement: “Every Stacktape stage is a complete, isolated stack — its own databases, functions, containers, and endpoints.” Similar claims appear in the first paragraph, PR previews, cost management, cleanup, and FAQ.
issues.reason: The provided source files show GitOps configurations include a `stage` field and deployments/deletions are orchestrated with that stage. They do not prove the broader resource-level claim that every stage always provisions separate databases, functions, containers, endpoints, buckets, or data. This claim is central to the page and should be grounded in stage/configuration sources, not the GitOps UI alone.
issues.suggestedFix: Either provide and cite the stage/deployment source that establishes stage isolation, or narrow the wording to: GitOps configurations deploy operations with a stage name; branch and PR patterns commonly use distinct stage names such as `prod`, `staging`, and `pr-{number}`.

issues.statement: “In the Console, create a GitOps configuration with ‘Push to Branch’ as the trigger, select the branch to watch, and set your desired stage name.” Also: “When creating a PR preview GitOps configuration in the Console, you can enable the ‘Delete stack after pull request is merged or closed’ option.”
issues.reason: The provided Console source confirms visible trigger labels `PR Opened` and `Push to Branch`, and a `New GitOps configuration` button, but it does not include the modal fields or the exact auto-delete option label. The server source has a `deleteStackAfterPrClose` field, but not this Console text or flow.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the grounded trigger labels, but avoid exact unprovided Console flow text and option label. For example: “GitOps configurations support `PR Opened` and `Push to Branch` triggers. PR preview configurations can carry a `deleteStackAfterPrClose` setting.”

issues.statement: “There is no Stacktape-imposed limit on the number of stages.”
issues.reason: No provided source states there is no Stacktape limit on stage count. This is a broad platform-limit claim, and the supplied GitOps source only shows querying and rendering Git deployment configurations, not limits or quotas.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the sentence or narrow it to AWS quota guidance without making a Stacktape limit claim. For example: “The provided GitOps source does not define a stage-count limit; in practice, many simultaneous stages can run into AWS service quotas.”

issues.statement: “You can set up all three as GitOps configurations in the Console without writing any pipeline files.”
issues.reason: The provided Console source shows GitOps configurations with only two trigger types: `PR Opened` and `Push to Branch`. It does not show wildcard branch support such as `release/*`, automatic feature-branch creation, or enough modal behavior to support “all three” patterns as Console configurations.
issues.suggestedFix: Say that the Console GitOps page supports `Push to Branch` and `PR Opened` configurations. Present feature branch stages as custom CI/manual deployments unless a source proves Console support for that exact pattern.

issues.statement: “PR closed or merged — If auto-delete is enabled, the handler starts a stack deletion for the preview stage and removes the temporary configuration.”
issues.reason: The supplied server source always returns deletion configurations for matching temporary PR configurations on `PULL_REQUEST_CLOSED`; the shown `startDeletion` function does not check `deleteStackAfterPrClose`. The source does show `deleteStackAfterPrClose` is stored on temporary PR configs, so another file may filter on it, but that behavior is not visible here.
issues.suggestedFix: Write cautiously: “PR close events are parsed as `PULL_REQUEST_CLOSED`; temporary PR configurations include a `deleteStackAfterPrClose` setting, and matching configurations can be used to start deletion.” Avoid saying the shown handler conditionally checks the setting unless another source proves it.

positiveFindings: The draft uses valid documentation routes from the provided navigation index; no invented cross-links were found.
The draft correctly identifies the two visible GitOps trigger labels as `PR Opened` and `Push to Branch`.
The draft’s `pr-{number}` stage naming for PR previews is grounded in `utils.ts`, which sets `stage: \`pr-${pullRequestNumber}\`` on PR-opened deployments.
The draft correctly links logs to `stacktape debug:logs` and `/cli/debug-logs`, matching the canonical command guidance.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is useful and mostly aligned with the GitOps source for PR-opened, push-to-branch, temporary PR configurations, and PR-close deletion. The main publication risks are unsupported Console workflow details, unsupported stage naming constraints, and several claims that go beyond the provided GitHub App and GitOps table sources.

issues.statement: Stage names must be lowercase alphanumeric and dashes only, minimum 2 characters, maximum 12 characters, with the 12-character maximum enforced in Console GitOps configurations.
issues.reason: The provided source files do not show any stage-name validation rules or length constraints. The GitOps table only displays a stage value or the PR placeholder; it does not enforce or document constraints.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the specific character and length table unless validation source is provided. Narrow the guidance to a best-practice recommendation such as: "Keep generated stage names short and stable, especially for PR previews, because Stacktape passes the stage into deployment operations and displays it in GitOps configurations."

issues.statement: Subsequent pushes create a temporary internal configuration for pushes to the PR branch, and push events update the same `pr-{number}` stage.
issues.reason: The temporary PR configuration creation is supported, but the draft omits an important source-grounded detail: regular push events explicitly exclude temporary PR configurations to prevent double deployments. Temporary PR configs are selected by pull request number and branch for PR-close cleanup, not by the ordinary push-to-branch lookup described broadly in the draft.
issues.suggestedFix: Clarify the mechanics narrowly: "After a PR-opened deployment, Stacktape creates a temporary Git deployment configuration marked `isTemporaryForPr` for the PR branch and `pr-{number}` stage. The GitHub App excludes temporary PR configs from ordinary push-to-branch matches to avoid double deployments."

issues.statement: GitOps vs custom CI/CD guidance does not distinguish CodeBuild-based runners from EC2 runners.
issues.reason: The human-maintained section instructions explicitly require CodeBuild-based runners and EC2 runners to be distinguished in CI/CD pages. The draft links to GitOps and custom CI/CD, but does not mention runner choice or point readers to the build runners page when discussing automated deployments.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a short runner-choice paragraph near the GitOps/custom CI sections: "Stacktape deployment automation can run through managed GitOps and can also be integrated with your own CI. For Stacktape-managed runners, distinguish CodeBuild-based runners from EC2 runners and choose based on isolation, startup behavior, and operational control; see /ci-cd-and-gitops/build-runners."

evidence.quote: Distinguish CodeBuild-based runners from EC2 runners explicitly.

positiveFindings: The draft correctly anchors PR preview stage naming to `pr-{number}`, which is directly supported by the GitHub App source.

The draft correctly describes PR close cleanup as conditional on a deletion configuration created for the PR flow; the source includes `deleteStackAfterPrClose`, temporary PR configs, and deletion orchestration on `PULL_REQUEST_CLOSED`.
The draft keeps Stacktape configuration examples out of fenced TypeScript/YAML blocks, so it does not violate the documented CodeBlock rules for Stacktape config examples.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Claims about PR stage naming (`pr-${pullRequestNumber}`), GitHub App comment lifecycle, and `deleteStackAfterPrClose` are directly grounded in the console-app source — no invented behavior.
Tables are used throughout to structure all multi-value comparisons (shapes, provider variables, cleanup strategies), making the page highly scannable and citation-ready.
Both the GitOps-managed path and the custom CI/CD path receive equivalent treatment with concrete commands, covering GitHub and GitLab separately.
The multi-provider environment variable reference table (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Bitbucket) is a genuinely practical artifact that saves readers significant research time.
The FAQ spans all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (GitLab support, stage naming), AWS use-case (costs, data on delete), and comparison (GitOps vs custom CI/CD).
Auto-delete recommendation is opinionated and carries a concrete reason (cost for idle databases and containers), not just 'you should clean up'.
No style violations: no YAML Stacktape config blocks, no StacktapeConfig type pattern, no defineConfig in fenced TypeScript blocks, no multi-command single bash blocks.
problems: 'When to use this pattern' and 'When NOT to use this pattern' are both H2 sections buried near the bottom of the page, after five major implementation H2s. A first-time reader landing on this page cannot assess fit without first reading through all three pattern deep-dives, naming conventions, cost management, and cleanup strategies. For an audience with low AWS experience trying to decide whether to adopt this pattern, this ordering is backwards.

The page offers no quick orientation for a new user who doesn't know where to begin — GitOps or custom CI/CD? The pattern has three shapes and two setup paths; a first-timer needs a one-sentence recommendation before diving in.

Feature branch deploy/delete examples omit `--autoConfirmOperation`, while PR preview examples include it. The flag is just as relevant for feature branch automation, and the inconsistency implies it's PR-preview-specific when it isn't.
optionalImprovements: Move 'When to use this pattern' and 'When NOT to use this pattern' to directly after the opening 'How the pattern works' overview section — before the Long-lived branch stages deep-dive. This lets first-time readers make the fit decision before investing time in implementation details.

Add a one-sentence opinionated recommendation near the top of the page (or in the opening paragraph) along the lines of: 'If your repository is on GitHub, start with GitOps in the Stacktape Console — no pipeline files needed. For GitLab, Bitbucket, or teams with existing pipelines, use the custom CI/CD path.' This eliminates a key decision point that currently requires reading to the bottom of the page.

Add `--autoConfirmOperation` to the feature branch `deploy` and `delete` examples for consistency with PR preview examples. The current omission implies the flag is optional in the PR context but unnecessary for feature branches, which is misleading.

Several FAQ answers run 5–6 sentences, above the 2-4 sentence target for AI extractability. The answers for 'How do I debug a failing PR preview deployment?' and 'Can I share a database between stages?' are good candidates for trimming without losing the essential information.

The page uses `npx stacktape` in all examples but never explains why (the CLI isn't globally installed by default in CI). A single parenthetical — '(or `stacktape` if installed globally)' — on the first example would remove a potential confusion point for readers setting up CI for the first time.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Source-grounded accuracy: PR stage name `pr-{number}`, `deleteStackAfterPrClose`, temporary GitOps config creation, and PR comment posting on deploy/failure are all directly verifiable in `utils.ts` and `GitOps.tsx`.
Shell command discipline is fully compliant: each CLI variant has its own fenced block with prose introducing it; no multi-command blocks with `#` comment separators.

Decision support is consistently present for every major variant — GitOps vs custom CI/CD, long-lived vs PR preview vs feature branch — with when-to-use, when-to-skip, cost signals, and explicit opinions.
The CI provider env-var table (GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket) is highly practical and saves reader research time.
When to use / When NOT to use are correctly structured as peer H2s, not nested.
FAQ spans all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS service use-case, comparison/choosing) with 10 grounded entries.
No forbidden patterns: no YAML Stacktape config, no `StacktapeConfig` type import, no fenced TypeScript `defineConfig` blocks, no marketing language.
problems: H2 'How the pattern works' is phrased as a verb-phrase sentence rather than the preferred 1–5 word noun-form label ('Overview' or 'Pattern overview'). Headings like 'Long-lived branch stages', 'Feature branch stages', 'Cost management' follow the noun-form convention correctly; this one is an outlier.

The page does not describe that subsequent commits pushed to an open PR branch also trigger re-deployments to the preview stage. This behavior is grounded in the source: `createGitConfigurationForPR` sets `deployOnGitEvent: 'PUSHED_TO_BRANCH'` with `isTemporaryForPr: true`, so every new commit to the PR branch re-deploys to `pr-{number}`. An engineer wondering 'if I push another commit, does the preview update?' won't find the answer.

The 'Auto-delete on PR close' sub-section presents `deleteStackAfterPrClose` as a user-facing toggle, but the provided source (`utils.ts` `getActionsToPerform`) doesn't show that flag being checked before adding configs to `deletionConfigurations` — the check presumably lives in the upstream handler not shown. The docs state this as settled behavior ('If deleteStackAfterPrClose is enabled'). This is likely correct but is slightly over-claimed given the visible source.
optionalImprovements: Rename the H2 'How the pattern works' to 'Overview' or 'Pattern overview' to match the noun-form convention used by all other H2s on the page.

Add a sentence in the 'How PR previews work with GitOps' step 2 (or a follow-up bullet) explaining that a temporary `PUSHED_TO_BRANCH` config is created so subsequent commits to the PR branch also re-deploy to the preview stage automatically — not just the initial PR open event.

Qualify the `deleteStackAfterPrClose` claim slightly: 'When `deleteStackAfterPrClose` is enabled on the original GitOps configuration, that setting is propagated to the temporary PR config, and the handler initiates stack deletion on PR close.' This keeps the claim grounded without removing useful guidance.

The 'Combining patterns' section ends with 'without writing any pipeline files' — worth adding a parenthetical reminder that this applies only to GitHub repositories, since the GitLab/GitHub distinction is already established earlier but the reader might skim to this section in isolation.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and suitable for search snippets.
The page has a practical structure: pattern overview, long-lived stages, PR previews, feature branches, naming, costs, cleanup, and FAQ.
Tables make the branch/stage mappings, provider variables, cleanup strategies, and combined patterns easy to scan.
Shell examples follow the copy-button rules: one command per fenced block and no explanatory comments inside command blocks.
The draft correctly distinguishes GitHub managed GitOps from custom CI/CD for GitLab and other providers, which matches the GitHub App source scope.
problems: Several Stacktape-specific claims are not grounded in the provided source files, including deployment identity by project/stage/account/region, AWS resource duplication per stage, stage names being incorporated into AWS resource names, and exact cleanup consequences for every resource type.

The GitOps Console workflow is described beyond the visible console source. The source supports the table columns, trigger labels, create button, and filtered non-temporary configs, but not detailed setup steps inside the modal.

The draft states GitOps PR previews create and remove temporary configurations, but the provided source only shows helper functions for creating/deleting PR configs; it does not show where or whether those helpers are always invoked in the event handler flow.

The draft claims PR preview deployment comments include status and that orchestration failure updates comments with failure details. The source supports pending comments and failure-update attempts, but the prose should be narrower about exactly what is guaranteed.

Build runner guidance contains likely useful distinctions between CodeBuild-managed and EC2 self-hosted runners, but no build-runner source is provided here, so those details are unsupported in this review context.

Cost guidance is useful but over-specific in places. Claims such as serverless previews costing nearly nothing, generous free tiers, and containers or relational databases always incurring idle cost are AWS-background claims, but they are not grounded in the provided Stacktape source and should be framed cautiously.

The FAQ includes several unsupported Stacktape-specific assertions: stage-specific configuration via defineConfig receiving stage name, sharing resources via $ResourceParam across stacks, connectTo environment variables for migration scripts, and managed GitOps being simpler because it has no pipeline files and automatic lifecycle management.
Some H2 first paragraphs are shorter or less self-contained than the AEO target, especially Feature branch stages, Combining patterns, When to use this pattern, and When NOT to use this pattern.

The page gives realistic commands, but examples omit required context that a user may need in CI, such as project/config path behavior and authentication. If those details are covered on linked pages, the draft should state that rather than implying the shown command is complete for every pipeline.
mandatoryFixes: Ground or remove unsupported Stacktape-specific claims. In particular, verify deployment identity, per-stage resource duplication, resource-name effects, deletion semantics, stage-specific configuration mechanics, $ResourceParam cross-stack usage, and connectTo migration environment variables against authoritative source files before publication.
Narrow the GitOps Console setup prose to what the provided console source supports, or add the CreateGitOpsConfigModal/server sources needed to support the setup fields and behavior.

Revise the PR preview lifecycle wording to match the GitHub App source exactly: PR opened and push events produce deployment configurations, PR opened overrides the branch to the PR head branch and stage to pr-{pullRequestNumber}, PR close finds a temporary push-to-branch config by PR branch and pull request number, and deletion orchestration is represented by startDeletion.

Remove or source the CodeBuild-vs-EC2 runner details. If the page keeps runner tradeoffs, include build-runner source files or link without asserting unsupported limits like predefined compute tiers, disk-size control, and pre-installed tooling.

Rewrite unsupported FAQ answers so each Stacktape-specific claim is either source-backed or clearly delegated to a linked page. Do not claim defineConfig receives the stage name, connectTo injects a migration connection string, or shared databases can be referenced via $ResourceParam unless the source for those facts is included.
optionalImprovements: Make each H2 opening paragraph 40-75 words and explicitly name the concept, for example “Feature branch stages are...” instead of relying on nearby context.
Add a short decision table comparing long-lived branch stages, PR previews, and feature branch stages before the detailed sections; the page already has the material and would become easier to cite.
Move the strongest recommendation earlier: most teams should use long-lived prod/staging plus PR previews, then add feature branch stages only for longer-running shared work.
For every CI variable table entry, add a caveat that provider variable names depend on event type and pipeline context, especially GitLab branch variables in merge request pipelines.
Consider replacing broad cost statements with a safer rule of thumb: every additional stage can create additional AWS resources, so preview cost depends on the resources included in that stage.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly consistent with the GitHub App and Console source, but it overstates several PR-preview and Console GitOps behaviors beyond what the provided files prove. The highest-risk issues are around PR-close deletion semantics, automatic lifecycle wording, stage-name configurability, and using `connectTo` env vars on a CI/CD page without source support.

issues.statement: “PR closed or merged — If the `deleteStackAfterPrClose` setting is enabled on the configuration, the handler starts a stack deletion for the preview stage and removes the temporary configuration.”
issues.reason: The provided GitHub App source does not gate creation of deletionConfigurations on `deleteStackAfterPrClose`; it finds matching temporary PR configurations on `PULL_REQUEST_CLOSED` and returns them for deletion handling. The only visible use of `deleteStackAfterPrClose` is copying the value into the temporary PR GitOps configuration when it is created. The draft’s conditional wording is not supported by the shown close-event selection logic.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the statement to what the source shows: on PR close, the handler looks up the temporary PR configuration for the PR branch/number and can pass it into deletion handling; the temporary configuration stores `deleteStackAfterPrClose`. Avoid claiming the exact gating behavior unless the orchestration file that checks this flag is included.

deleteStackAfterPrClose,

issues.statement: “Every push to that branch triggers a deployment to the named stage.”
issues.reason: The push-event handler explicitly ignores branch deletion events and push events that do not include a commit SHA. The draft’s “every push” wording is broader than the source behavior.
issues.suggestedFix: Say “A push event with a branch ref and commit SHA can trigger deployment to the configured stage.” If keeping reader-facing wording, use “pushes to the configured branch trigger deployments” without “every,” and avoid implying deleted-branch or malformed push events deploy.

issues.statement: “The stage name `pr-{number}` is derived automatically from the PR number by the GitHub App handler… This naming is built into the handler and is not configurable through the GitOps UI.”
issues.reason: The source supports the automatic `pr-${pullRequestNumber}` stage for PR-opened events, but the provided UI source does not prove the naming is not configurable in the GitOps UI. The table renders `gitDeploymentConfig.stage || 'pr-{#number}'`, which suggests PR configs may display a placeholder when no stage is stored, but it does not establish a non-configurability constraint.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the grounded part: “For PR-opened GitOps events, the handler sets the temporary stage to `pr-{number}`.” Remove “not configurable through the GitOps UI” unless the CreateGitOpsConfigModal source is included and proves it.

evidence.quote:
label: gitDeploymentConfig.stage || 'pr-{#number}',
              value: gitDeploymentConfig.stage || 'Z'

issues.statement: “The GitHub App handler automates the preview lifecycle: 1. PR opened — The handler starts a deployment… 2. Temporary configuration — A temporary GitOps configuration is created… 3. PR closed or merged — …”
issues.reason: The source file shows separate helper functions for selecting actions, starting deployments, creating temporary PR configurations, starting deletions, and deleting temporary configurations. It does not show the orchestration sequence that calls these functions or proves the exact order presented in the draft.
issues.suggestedFix: Rephrase as source-grounded capabilities rather than an exact sequence: “The GitHub App code derives PR events, starts deployments for PR previews, creates temporary PR GitOps configurations, and has helpers to delete preview stages and temporary configurations on PR close.”

issues.statement: “The GitOps page in the Stacktape Console lists configurations by deployment trigger, branch, stage name, AWS region, and AWS account.”
issues.reason: The provided Console component shows the table columns and values, but it does not establish the page label “GitOps page” or a user-facing Console navigation location. The source-grounding rules require Console UI flows and locations to be grounded in console-app source, not inferred.
issues.suggestedFix: Avoid naming a Console page location unless route/navigation source is provided. Use “The Console GitOps table displays deployment trigger, branch, stage name, region, and AWS account.”

issues.statement: “The migration connects using the preview's database connection string, available via `connectTo` environment variables.”
issues.reason: The page source list contains no Stacktape config type or `ResourceAccessProps.connectTo` documentation. The draft makes a specific Stacktape env-var injection claim on a CI/CD pattern page without source support.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace with a link-level statement that does not restate env-var behavior: “Use the connection mechanism documented in [Connecting resources](/configuration/connecting-resources) for the preview stage’s database.” If retaining env-var details, include the relevant `connectTo` source and use the `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM]` pattern.

issues.statement: “On orchestration failure, the PR comment is updated with failure details.”
issues.reason: The source supports failure-comment updates only for preview deployments/deletions where `pullRequestNumber` is present, and even then the update is attempted inside a nested try/catch that may fail. The draft states it as unconditional behavior.
issues.suggestedFix: Say “For preview deployments and deletions, the handler attempts to update the PR comment with failure details if orchestration fails.”

issues.statement: “The `defineConfig` function receives the stage name, so you can branch on it directly to customize resources per stage.”
issues.reason: This may be true elsewhere in Stacktape, but no `defineConfig` type/source file is included in this page’s source list. Because the page is specifically about CI/CD stage mapping, this claim should be linked or sourced from configuration docs rather than asserted from the GitHub App and Console files provided here.
issues.suggestedFix: Either remove the `defineConfig` callback-detail claim or rewrite as “Use the stage-specific patterns documented in [Stages and environments](/configuration/stages-and-environments) to customize preview resources.”

positiveFindings: The draft uses valid documentation routes from the provided navigation index; no invented cross-links were found.
The draft correctly distinguishes Stacktape-managed GitOps from custom CI/CD and explicitly discusses CodeBuild-based runners versus EC2 runners, matching the section instruction.
The PR preview stage naming claim `pr-${pullRequestNumber}` is grounded in the GitHub App source, aside from the unsupported non-configurability statement.
The Console table columns for deployment trigger, branch, stage name, region, and AWS account are grounded in the provided GitOps component source.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly consistent with the supplied GitHub App and Console GitOps sources, especially around PR-stage naming, temporary PR configurations, and the two visible GitOps trigger labels. The main problems are over-specific Console flow claims, a few unsupported CLI/config assertions, and one likely wrong claim about how `defineConfig` receives stage data.

issues.statement: “The `defineConfig` function receives the stage name, so you can branch on it directly to customize resources per stage.”
issues.reason: The supplied sources do not include Stacktape config API types or `defineConfig` behavior. This is a Stacktape-specific API claim that would change how readers write config, but it is not grounded in the provided GitHub App or Console GitOps files.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace with narrower wording grounded in the page’s sources, such as: “Use stage-specific configuration to keep preview stages smaller than production.” If the page needs the exact `defineConfig` API shape, add the relevant Stacktape config type source and verify it there.

positiveFindings: The draft correctly grounds PR preview stage naming: the GitHub App source sets `stage: `pr-${pullRequestNumber}`` for `PULL_REQUEST_OPENED` events.

The draft correctly describes temporary PR GitOps configurations: the source creates a Push to Branch configuration for the PR branch with `pullRequestNumber`, `pullRequestTargetBranch`, `deleteStackAfterPrClose`, and `isTemporaryForPr: true`.
The draft correctly says the Console GitOps table shows trigger, branch, stage, region, and AWS account columns.
The draft correctly uses documented routes from the supplied navigation index; no invented cross-links were found.

The draft does not violate the forbidden Stacktape config example patterns: there are no hand-authored YAML Stacktape configs, no `StacktapeConfig` typed examples, and no fenced TypeScript Stacktape config examples.

verifierResults.summary: I found 3 medium issues. Because the page metadata has no primary typeName, a normal property-by-property API completeness audit was not possible; the findings focus on source-visible GitOps configuration behavior and claims that affect how readers configure branch-to-stage deployments.

issues.statement: The long-lived branch mapping table includes `release/*` as a branch pattern mapped to a `release` stage.
issues.reason: The provided GitHub App source matches GitOps configurations by an exact branch string from the webhook event. The source does not show wildcard branch matching for managed GitOps, so `release/*` reads like an unsupported Console pattern.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace `release/*` with a concrete branch example such as `release-2026-05` or state that wildcard branch routing belongs in custom CI/CD where the pipeline derives the `--stage` value itself.

evidence.quote: branches={projectDetails.branches?.map((branch) => branch.name)}

issues.statement: “Unlike PR previews, feature stages don't auto-create from a trigger — you deploy them explicitly when you need a live environment for a long-running feature.”
issues.reason: The source shows a managed `PUSHED_TO_BRANCH` GitOps trigger. A feature branch stage can be updated automatically from a configured exact branch; what is not shown is automatic catch-all creation for arbitrary feature branches.
issues.suggestedFix: Revise to: “Feature branch stages can be deployed explicitly from custom CI/CD, or updated automatically by a Push to Branch GitOps configuration for a specific branch. Managed GitOps does not show a wildcard ‘every feature branch’ rule in the provided source.”

evidence.quote: if (gitEvent.eventType === 'PULL_REQUEST_OPENED' || gitEvent.eventType === 'PUSHED_TO_BRANCH')

issues.statement: The GitOps setup sections do not mention the Stacktape config path carried by a GitOps configuration.
issues.reason: The deployment source passes `configPath` into `orchestrateOperation`, and temporary PR configurations copy the same `configPath`. Readers with monorepos or non-default config locations need to know that the GitOps configuration includes a config path used for both the initial PR deployment and the temporary PR configuration.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a short caveat in the GitOps setup section: “A GitOps configuration also carries the Stacktape config path. If your `stacktape.ts` is not at the default project location, set the config path in the GitOps configuration; PR preview temporary configurations copy that path.”

configPath,

    isTemporaryForPr: true,

positiveFindings: The draft correctly distinguishes managed GitOps from custom CI/CD and explicitly separates CodeBuild-based runners from EC2 runners at the points where runners are discussed.
The draft correctly names the two user-facing GitOps trigger labels shown in the Console source: PR Opened and Push to Branch.
The PR preview stage naming claim `pr-{number}` is grounded in the GitHub App source, which sets `stage: \`pr-${pullRequestNumber}\`` for PR-opened deployments.

The draft avoids the forbidden Stacktape config patterns: there are no hand-authored Stacktape YAML examples, no plain-object `StacktapeConfig` examples, and no fenced TypeScript Stacktape config blocks.
